intro: Hey, welcome back to The Weekly Rundown, where we've got everything from courtroom drama in Monaco to a Wimbledon champion's world getting flipped upside down — yeah, it's been *that* kind of week. Grab a snack, because the Jets are finally being put to work, the Cubs brought a broom to New York, and Rory McIlroy apparently has a golden ticket that's rubbing some people the wrong way.

stories.headline: Jets Fans, Your Offseason Homework Is Due
stories.body: The Jets have been busy this offseason, and cornerback Nahshon Wright is already hyping up the moves — always a good sign when the players are excited before a snap is taken. One Jets prospect even cracked an NFL All-UDFA team, which is either a promising sign of depth or a reminder that the roster still has some question marks. Hey, optimism is free.

stories.headline: Maliq Brown Lands in San Antonio and Feels Right at Home
stories.body: The young forward is settling into the Spurs system, and by all accounts, the fit feels natural — almost like he's been there before. San Antonio has a way of doing that, quietly building a culture that just works for developing talent. Don't sleep on this one.

stories.headline: NBA Vet Sounds the Alarm: Treat Caitlin Clark Better or Lose Everything
stories.body: An 11-year NBA veteran is speaking up for Clark, arguing that the WNBA is essentially playing with fire by letting the rough treatment of its biggest star continue unchecked. Clark has single-handedly moved the needle for women's basketball in a way nobody else has in years. Biting the hand that feeds you is never a great strategy.

stories.headline: Bulls Rookie Caleb Wilson Has a List — and It Has Three Names On It
stories.body: Wilson isn't shy about his chip on his shoulder after being the fourth pick in the 2026 Draft, and he's already locked in on proving the teams that passed on him wrong. Chicago fans have seen this kind of motivated energy before, and honestly, it's exactly the kind of attitude you want from a young guy entering a rebuild. Bulletin board material, secured.

stories.headline: Cubs Broom the Mets Right Out of Town
stories.body: Dansby Swanson and Pete Crow-Armstrong were the stars of the show as Chicago swept New York in four straight, which is the kind of week that makes Cubs fans forget every bad April they've ever lived through. The Mets, meanwhile, are having exactly the kind of season where a four-game sweep feels less like a surprise and more like a Tuesday. More on them in a second.

stories.headline: Mets Fire Mendoza, But the House Was Already on Fire
stories.body: Carlos Mendoza is out as Mets manager, taking the fall for a brutal 34-47 record — but let's be honest, no skipper was steering that roster to a pennant. When your problems are baked into the roster construction, changing the guy in the dugout is more PR move than fix. Mets fans have seen this movie before and they know how it ends.

stories.headline: Cooper Ingle Earns His Call-Up the Hard Way
stories.body: The Cleveland Guardians called up catcher Cooper Ingle this week, and his path to the bigs is a genuinely good story — he committed to a serious nutrition overhaul and locked in on the little stuff between games, and it paid off. The Guardians lineup has been scuffling, so they could use the fresh energy. Here's hoping the kid gets a hit in his first at-bat so we can all feel good about it.

stories.headline: Brendan Sorsby Finds Every Pro Football Door Locked After Betting Scandal
stories.body: Texas Tech QB Brendan Sorsby, who admitted to gambling on his own college team, has now been shut out of both the NFL and the CFL — so pretty much every professional football option on the continent is off the table. It's a stunning fall for a guy who had real pro prospects, and a pretty clear message from both leagues that this kind of thing is a hard line. The story keeps getting worse with every news cycle.

stories.headline: UVA's Athletic Department Is Getting Put on Blast by Its Own Lacrosse Coach
stories.body: Lars Tiffany, one of the most successful lacrosse coaches in the country, is taking UVA to court — and the details of his lawsuit are painting a pretty unflattering picture of how the athletic department operates behind the scenes. Whatever the legal outcome ends up being, this kind of internal dispute going public is never a good look for a program. Grab some popcorn, because this one's going to be messy.

stories.headline: New Mexico State's 2026 Schedule Is Here — Time to Get to Know the Aggies
stories.body: New Mexico State heads into 2026 for Tony Sanchez's third season at the helm, and we've got a full breakdown of every opponent they'll face this fall. The Aggies have been quietly building something in Las Cruces, so this is a good time to get familiar with the schedule and see what kind of tests are coming their way. Could be a make-or-break year for the Sanchez era.

stories.headline: Is the World Cup Slowly Killing the Game It's Supposed to Celebrate?
stories.body: There's a growing chorus of voices worried that football's biggest stage is actually doing long-term damage to the sport itself — think commercialization, bloated formats, and the slow creep of American sports entertainment culture. Whether you think that's alarmist or overdue criticism probably depends on how you felt watching the 2026 tournament. Either way, it's a conversation that isn't going away.

stories.headline: Alexi Lalas Is Terrible at His Job — and Fox Sports Couldn't Be Happier
stories.body: If you've been watching the World Cup on Fox and wondering how Alexi Lalas keeps getting microphone time despite being almost aggressively wrong about everything, the answer is: that's kind of the whole point. Fox doesn't want nuanced tactical breakdowns — they want loud, quotable, argument-starting television, and Lalas delivers that in spades. Love him or hate him, you're still talking about him.

stories.headline: Spurs Snag a Goalkeeper — So Kinsky Can Finally Take the Wheel
stories.body: Tottenham have quietly signed Martin Dubravka as their fourth summer addition, and the move is less about Dubravka himself and more about what it signals: Antonin Kinsky is now the guy between the sticks at Spurs. Bringing in a veteran backup to take the pressure off a young starter is smart squad building, assuming Ange's lot can stay healthy long enough to test the theory.

stories.headline: Monaco's Result Isn't Over — See You in Court
stories.body: Turns out finishing a race doesn't necessarily mean you've won it. Two teams are taking their Monaco grievances to the courts just as the paddock rolls into Austria, which is either a bold legal strategy or the most dramatic way to spend a race week. The Styrian Mountains are lovely this time of year — less lovely when your lawyers are busy filing paperwork.

stories.headline: Le Mans Legend Gets His Shot in an F1 Car
stories.body: Ryo Hirakawa, who has essentially done everything you can do in endurance racing, is stepping into Esteban Ocon's Haas seat for his first 2026 grand prix outing. It's a fun crossover moment — the kind of driver who's spent years mastering 24-hour slogs now has to recalibrate his brain for 90-minute sprints. No pressure, but everyone's watching.

stories.headline: Austria Is On — And the Vibes Are Immaculate
stories.body: The Austrian Grand Prix is here, and honestly the Red Bull Ring in the Styrian Mountains might be the most picturesque stop on the entire calendar. It's also one of the shorter, punchier circuits, which tends to produce chaotic, entertaining racing — exactly what the sport needs after a week spent arguing in courtrooms about Monaco.

stories.headline: Vondrousova Blindsided by Four-Year Ban: 'I Was Completely Broken'
stories.body: Just three years after lifting the Wimbledon trophy, Marketa Vondrousova is facing a four-year doping suspension after refusing a drug test last December. She says the news left her feeling utterly destroyed, and honestly, the tennis world is right there with her — this came out of nowhere. Whether you're a fan or a skeptic, a four-year ban is a long time to sit on the sideline and think about what went wrong.

stories.headline: Columbus Is On Fire (And Not The Good Kind)
stories.body: Both Zach Werenski and Kirill Marchenko have made it clear they want out of Columbus, which is a rough look for a franchise still trying to convince people the rebuild is going well. The Blue Jackets did snag a promising power forward in the first round, but good luck getting anyone to talk about that when your two best players are basically waving goodbye from the podium.

stories.headline: Pittsburgh Drafted One Ruck Twin — Now Comes The Awkward Part
stories.body: The Penguins took Liam Ruck in the first round, which is great, except now his identical twin Markus is sitting there in round two and the whole thing has the energy of only inviting one sibling to the party. The twins did a joint interview about it, which is either adorable or a very subtle pressure campaign on Pittsburgh's front office — probably both.

stories.headline: The Wild Watched Round One Like The Rest Of Us, But Bill Guerin Is Cooking
stories.body: Minnesota stayed quiet on draft night, passing on a first-round pick, but GM Bill Guerin came out of it sounding like a man with a plan — his 'tomorrow's a new day' quote has big 'I've already made three calls' energy. With the Wild in a pivotal window, expect Guerin to be one of the more active names when the phones start buzzing on day two.

stories.headline: Rory Gets a Special Pass, and Not Everyone Is Happy About It
stories.body: The PGA Tour quietly invoked a little-known commissioner's rule to give Rory McIlroy some flexibility around his membership requirements, and predictably, not everyone is thrilled. Billy Horschel — never one to zip it — was among the first to sound off, and suspended players are also speaking up about what they see as a double standard. When you're the Tour's golden boy, apparently the rulebook comes with some fine print.

stories.headline: Ina Yoon Is Running Away With the KPMG, and Nelly Korda Is Chasing
stories.body: Ina Yoon holds a five-shot lead heading into the weekend at the KPMG Women's PGA Championship, looking completely unbothered on the biggest stage. Nelly Korda is six back, which is a lot of ground to make up but also exactly the kind of deficit that keeps Saturday interesting. If you're not watching this one, you probably should be.

stories.headline: Phil Mickelson Allegations Are Serious, and They're Not Going Away
stories.body: Investigative golf journalist Alan Shipnuck — who has never shied away from the Phil Mickelson beat — has published a report detailing two incidents in which Mickelson allegedly made unwanted public sexual advances, based on interviews with 19 people. This is a developing and deeply serious story, and it adds to an already lengthy list of controversies surrounding the six-time major champion. We'll be watching how the Tour and sponsors respond.
